  2. Once a file has been added, it will be visible within the pop up and can be downloaded.

Mark Tasks as Complete

  1. Once a task is finished, click on circle in front of the task to mark it complete.

  1. This will update the progress (7/12 Completed) at the top of the checklist and the person who completed the task will appear in the Completed By column.
  2. To view the date and time of completion, hover over the name of the person who completed the task.

Tasks can also be unmarked complete. When unmarked, the Completed by Name and timestamp will be removed.

Reuse your Checklist Next Month

Now that you've developed a top notch month end close checklist, you can re-use it each month with these simple steps:

In the upper right corner, select the 3 Dots and then click Copy & Create New Month. This will create your next month book close period.

All tasks will be copied with the following changes:

  • The Due date will be +1 month from the prior checklist.
  • The task will be in an incomplete state and all Completed By will be blank in the new month.
  • Attachments will not carry forward.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting

Journal Entries

Create a Journal EntryUploading Journal EntriesReversing Journal EntriesJournal Entry Approvals

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Journal Entries

Create a Journal Entry

Creating a journal entry in the Account Register lets you manually record financial transactions that the system doesn’t capture automatically. You can use this tool to adjust balances or record specific financial events. Proper use of journal entries ensures your accounts stay accurate and up to date.

There are two ways to access journal entries:

  1. Access Journal Entries either through:
    1. Go to Close Management > Account Register.

    1. Keyboard Shortcut:
      1. Press Cmd+K (Mac) or Ctrl+K (Windows) to open the Command Palette.
      2. Click the New Journal Entry option.

  1. Click + Add Journal Entry at the top of the screen.

  1. Fill in the required header level fields:
    1. Name: Enter a descriptive name for the entry (e.g., "Year-End Adjustment").
    2. Date: Enter the date of your entry (e.g., 12/23/2024).
    3. Reversal Date (optional): Enter a reversal date if applicable (e.g., MM/DD/YYYY). 📄 Reversing Journal Entries.

What is a Reversing Entry? A reversing entry is made at the beginning of a new accounting period to cancel out specific entries from the previous period. It helps avoid keeping accruals in the accounting system longer than necessary. By marking an original journal entry as a reversing entry, the software automatically creates the reversal in the next period, reducing the risk of manual errors.

    1. Attachment URL (optional): Paste any relevant links or documents.
    2. Customer/Vendor (optional): Select the customer or vendor from the dropdown list, if applicable.
  1. Enter transaction line details:
    1. Account: Select the relevant account for the transaction line from the dropdown menu.
    2. Debit/Credit: Enter a debit or credit value in the corresponding field (e.g., $5,000.00).

Note: Debit and Credit must be equal for the entry to balance.

    1. (Optional) Description: Provide a brief explanation of the transaction line (e.g., "Adjusting Q4 Revenue").
    2. Department: Select the department related to the transaction from the dropdown.
  1. If you need to enter more accounts, click Add New Line and repeat the previous steps (Account, Debit, Credit, Description, Department).

Note: Ensure to review the entered details to ensure accuracy before proceeding to the next step.

  1. (Optional) Click on the Attach Files button in the screen's upper right corner to attach files to your journal entry. Select the files you wish to upload from your device.
  2. Click Create Entry to complete and save the journal entry.

After creating the journal entry, you will see a message “Journal Entry created successfully,” confirming that the entry has been saved correctly.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Journal Entries

Uploading Journal Entries

To Upload journal entries into Rillet, navigate to Close Management > Account Register and select Upload Journal Entries

You can either download the journal entry template or just directly upload if you have already populated a template.

In the template fill out the following fields:

Header

Required?

Level

Description

Date

Yes

Header

The effective date of the JE. Date formats accepted are YYYY-MM-DD, MM/DD/YYYY, or DD/MM/YYYY

JE Name

Yes

Header

Top level description of the JE

Debit Amount

Yes

Line

Debit impact of the JE Line

Credit Amount

Yes

Line

Credit impact of the JE line

Currency

Yes

Header

Currency of the JE

FX Rate

Header

Optional override of the default FX rate

Account Number or Name

Yes

Line

The GL account related to the JE line. You can use either the account number (recommended) or the account name.

Description

No

Line

Optional line level description

Vendor Name

No

Header

Vendor related to the JE, if applicable. Cannot be used if customer is used.

Customer Name

No

Header

Customer related to the JE, if applicable. Cannot be used if vendor is used.

Reversal Date

No

Header

Optional JE reversal date.

Field {field_name}

No

Line

See below for more details

Custom Fields - Field {field_name}

When using custom Fields in Rillet for Department, Segment etc. you can upload the Field values on the journal entry template. Follow these easy steps:

  1. Update the template header for the name of your field. For example, if you have a Field in Rillet called "Department" the Template header should be Field Department
  2. Add additional Columns for other Fields
  3. Add Field Values on the Lines

Example:

Protips:

  • Upload the JE in CSV format
  • Use the General text format for all fields except for Date
  • Each line can only have a Debit or Credit Amount, cannot have both

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Journal Entries

Reversing Journal Entries

Reversal journal entries are entries made to reverse or negate a previous journal entry. These entries are typically used to ensure that accruals or deferrals from a previous period do not affect the current period's financial statements.

A common example of reversal journal entries is for accrued expenses at month end for any expenses which have been incurred, but no invoice or bill has been received.

To create a reversal journal entry in Rillet, navigate to New Journal Entry in the Command Palette (Ctrl+K or Cmd+K)

Once in the journal entry form, input the standard information and set the Reversal Date to the day you would like to create the entry which will completely reverse

When saved, this journal entry will create a second entry where the debits and credits are exactly the opposite of the original entry. You will see that the original journal entry is also linked in the Related items on the Reversal JE.

If the original journal entry is updated, the reversal JE is also update. Therefore in order to edit the reversal JE, you must update the original JE.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Journal Entries

Journal Entry Approvals

The journal entry approval process allows users to review and approve manual journal entries. This ensures that only specific people check and approve the entries before they are finalized.

Note: A journal entry must first be created before it can be approved. See the📄 Creating a Journal Entry guide for instructions.

To approve a journal entry:

  1. Go to Reporting in the main menu.
  2. Under Close Reports, select General Ledger.

  1. Filter the report to display all journal entries with a status of "Pending Approval." This will show entries that are awaiting review.

  1. Click the journal entry you want to approve.

Note: If you created the journal entry, you can edit it while it's unapproved, but only another user can approve it.

Important: Once the journal entry is approved, it can now be edited. After making the necessary changes, it will be sent back to Pending Approval.

  1. Click the Approve button if the entry meets the requirements. If it doesn’t, select the option to delete it.

After submitting your journal entry, you will see a message “Journal Entry approved successfully,” indicating that the approval process has been completed correctly.

FAQ

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. VAT

Understand VAT and Sales Taxes

VAT (Value Added Tax) and Sales Tax are both types of consumption taxes, meaning they are taxes imposed on the sale of goods and services. However, they differ in how and when they are collected, and how they are applied in the supply chain.

VAT (Value Added Tax)

VAT is a multi-stage tax that is applied at each stage of the production and distribution process. This means that every time a product is sold or a service is provided, the tax is added to the price. However, businesses that collect VAT on their sales are generally allowed to deduct the VAT they paid on their own purchases (this is called input tax). This process ensures that only the value added at each stage of production or service is taxed.

Key Features of VAT:

  • Applied at every stage: VAT is charged at each step in the supply chain (e.g., manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to consumer).
  • Tax credit mechanism: Businesses can reclaim VAT paid on their own purchases, making the tax neutral for them.
  • End consumer bears the cost: Ultimately, the final consumer pays the VAT, as businesses pass on the cost.
  • Rates vary: VAT rates vary between countries and can differ for different types of goods and services.

Examples:

  1. A manufacturer buys raw materials and pays VAT on them.
  2. The manufacturer adds value to the raw materials and sells the finished product to a wholesaler, charging VAT.
  3. The wholesaler then sells the product to a retailer, charging VAT again.
  4. Finally, the retailer sells it to the final consumer, and the consumer pays the VAT included in the price.

In this process, the VAT paid by businesses on purchases is deducted from the VAT they charge to customers, so the tax is ultimately passed on to the consumer.

Sales Tax

Sales tax, on the other hand, is typically a single-stage tax that is applied only at the final point of sale, when the product or service is sold to the consumer. Unlike VAT, businesses in the sales tax system do not get to deduct taxes they paid on their purchases.

Key Features of Sales Tax:

  • Applied at the final sale: The tax is charged only at the point of sale to the end consumer, not at each stage of the supply chain.
  • No tax credit: Businesses do not get to reclaim the tax paid on their inputs; they only collect the tax on their sales.
  • End consumer pays the tax: The business collects the tax and remits it to the government, but the end consumer bears the full cost of the tax.
  • Rates vary by jurisdiction: Sales tax rates can vary by state, region, or country.

Example:

  1. A retailer purchases products from a wholesaler without any tax being added.
  2. When the retailer sells the products to the final consumer, the retailer adds sales tax to the price.
  3. The consumer pays the price plus the sales tax, and the retailer remits the tax to the government.

Key Differences Between VAT and Sales Tax:

  • Collection Points: VAT is collected at each stage of the supply chain, while sales tax is only collected at the final point of sale to the consumer.
  • Tax Credit System: VAT allows businesses to reclaim tax paid on inputs (purchase tax credits), whereas sales tax does not.
  • Impact on Business: With VAT, businesses act as intermediaries, collecting tax at each stage, while in a sales tax system, businesses only collect tax on the final sale.
  • Complexity: VAT can be more complex due to its multi-stage nature and the need for businesses to track input and output tax. Sales tax tends to be simpler but can become complex when dealing with different rates in different regions.

Both taxes ultimately impact the price of goods and services for consumers, but their structure and the way they are implemented can vary significantly depending on the country and tax system in place.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. VAT

VAT Taxes in Rillet

Value Added Tax (VAT) is applied in countries such as the United Kingdom and various European nations. Unlike the U.S., where sales tax is charged only at the final sale, VAT is included at each stage of the production and sales process.

Invoices

When creating an invoice in Rillet, VAT can be added by toggling the tax option.

Customers can choose between:

  • Exclusive VAT: The entered amount is net, and VAT is added separately.
  • Inclusive VAT: The total amount includes VAT, meaning the net value is calculated accordingly.

Example:

  • Exclusive VAT: If you enter £100 and VAT is 20%, the total invoice will be £120.
  • Inclusive VAT: If you enter £100 as the total, the net amount before VAT is £83.33.

Contracts

Contracts in Rillet allow you to add VAT similarly to invoices. When creating a contract, you can activate the VAT option and select the appropriate type.

Once finalized, the VAT amount is automatically included in the invoice generated from the contract.

Quick Entries

In quick entries, the system defaults to inclusive VAT, as the total transaction amount cannot increase when using exclusive VAT.

To use VAT in a quick entry:

  1. Go to Cash Reconciliation.
  2. Select Bank Transaction > Quick Entry.
  3. In the dropdown, select the VAT type.
  4. Click Create and Match.
  5. Select Matched to view the linked bank transaction and Rillet transaction.

Note:

    • Quick entries always use inclusive VAT because the total transaction amount remains fixed.
    • In this case, if the total transaction amount is $37.62, using exclusive VAT would increase the expense, which is not allowed.

Journal Entry

Rillet allows users to include VAT when creating journal entries. See the general process for creating a journal entry and when dealing with VAT, consider the following:

  • VAT Type: Select the appropriate VAT rate from the dropdown menu.
  • Select Between Exclusive and Inclusive VAT:
    • Exclusive VAT: Adds a separate tax line item to the entry.

Note: The system automatically reflects this adjustment, so if you input a net amount of £100 with a VAT of £20, the total will be presented as £120 without needing manual adjustments.

    • Inclusive VAT: Applies a VAT tax reclassification that automatically adjusts the amounts.

Note: The total amount entered already includes VAT, streamlining the entry process.

Verify the debit and credit amounts to ensure the entry balances. Once finalized, VAT will be reflected in the relevant accounts.

Automated Accounting Records in Rillet

Rillet automates the accounting records of transactions with VAT. When invoices or contracts are created, the system automatically reflects VAT in the respective accounts, such as accounts receivable or accounts payable. This ensures accurate financial reporting and compliance with VAT regulations.

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Chart of Accounts

Set Up Your Chart of Accounts

Setting up your Chart of Accounts helps organize your financial data to make it easier to track and manage transactions. It allows you to categorize financial activities, which helps to produce accurate financial reports, like income statements and balance sheets.

A clear account structure streamlines the understanding of your business's financial health and ensures everything is properly accounted. Whether you’re creating your Chart of Accounts for the first time or revising an existing setup, it provides the foundation for clear financial management and reporting.

To Set Up Your Chart of Accounts:

  1. Click on your organization’s logo from the bottom left corner.

  1. Select Organization Settings > Chart of Accounts.
  2. Click + Add Account at the top-right of the page.

  1. Enter the account details:
    • Number: Assign the account number (e.g., "1000").
    • Name: Enter a specific and descriptive account name. (e.g., "Bank Account").
    • Type: Select the account type from the dropdown menu. Options include:
      • Asset
      • Equity
      • Expense
      • Liability
      • Income
  2. Subtype: The options depend on the selected type.

Type

Subtype Options

Asset

Accumulated Depreciation, Allowance for Doubtful Accounts, Bank, Deferred Commissions, Fixed Assets, Other Assets, Other Current Asset, Prepaid Expense

Equity

Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income, Additional Paid In Capital, Equity, Equity, Compensation

Expense

Cost of Goods Sold, Non Operating Income, Non Operating Expense, Operating Expense, Operating Expense – Commissions, Operating Expense – Office Expenses, Operating Expense – Personnel, Operating Expense – Sales & Marketing

Liability

Accrued Expense, Credit Card, Customer Deposits, Debt, Lease Liability, Other Current Liability, Other Long Term Liability

Income

Revenue

  1. Optional fields (if applicable):
    • Prepaid Account: Appears if Type is Expense
    • Parent Grouping: Use this to organize the account in categories (e.g., group multiple bank accounts under "Assets").
    • Link to a connected bank account: Select one of your bank accounts to connect to the one you are creating. Appears if:
      • Type is Asset and Subtype is Bank.
      • Type is Liability and Subtype is Credit Card.
  2. Click Save to create the account to complete the process.

After creating the account, you will see a message stating “Account created” confirming that the account has been saved correctly.

See also:

  • Manage Account Groupings
  • Deactivating an Account
  • Best Practices to Structure your Chart of Accounts

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Chart of Accounts

Manage Account Groupings

Parent grouping is a feature that allows you to create a hierarchical structure for your accounts, grouping them based on account types. This provides an extra level of organization within your Chart of Accounts, helping you categorize and manage your accounts more efficiently.

In other ERPs this is commonly called "Parent Accounts"

Delete a Grouping

If a grouping is no longer necessary, it can be deleted or removed from the group to exclude it from financial reports like the income statement.

To delete a grouping:

  1. Move the accounts to another grouping and make sure none is under the grouping you want to delete.
  2. Select the three-dot button next to the grouping you want to delete.
  3. Select Delete Group.

Viewing Parent Groupings in Financial Statements

The Parent Groupings you've set up in your Chart of Accounts will appear in the Balance Sheet to provide an organized, hierarchical structure for reporting. This allows you to easily categorize and view your accounts under appropriate headings like Assets, Liabilities, and Equity.

To View Parent Grouping in the Balance Sheet:

  1. Go to Reports in the main menu.
  2. Select Balance Sheet.

  1. Review the Groupings The Balance Sheet will display your accounts grouped according to the parent groupings you've created. These groupings will appear under significant categories such as:
    • Assets Current Assets, Non-Current Assets
    • Liabilities Current Liabilities, Non-Current Liabilities
    • Equity

  1. Expand/Collapse the Groupings
    • You can expand or collapse the sections under each parent grouping to see individual accounts.
    • For example, under Current Assets, you might see Cash, Accounts Receivable, and Prepaid Expenses as individual accounts, each belonging to their respective parent grouping.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Chart of Accounts

Deactivate an Account

Deactivating an account in Rillet means that the account can no longer be used for new transactions but remains in the system for historical reporting.

Before You Start

Deactivating an account will include the following features:

  1. Prevents Future Entries – The account will no longer be available for journal entries, invoices, or other transactions.
  2. Keeps Historical Data Intact – Past transactions remain in reports and financial statements.
  3. Affect on Open Transactions – If the account is linked to recurring transactions, integrations, or automated processes such as active contracts, then the account will continue to post until those open transactions have run out.
  4. Does Not Delete the Account – Unlike deletion, deactivation ensures compliance and audit trail integrity.

Deactivate an Account

To deactivate an account:

  1. Click your company's logo on the lower left corner.
  2. Select Organization Settings > Chart of Accounts.
  3. Click the three-dot button next to the account you want to deactivate.
  4. Click Deactivate.

After that, you will instantly receive a message to confirm that the account has been deactivated.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Chart of Accounts

Why does my General Ledger balance not tie to the subledger?

Discrepancies between the General Ledger (GL) and subledgers are relatively common. One of the most frequent causes is manual journal entries posted directly to the GL that are not reflected in the related subledger or schedule.

Common Scenario

If the Deferred Revenue balance on the Balance Sheet doesn't match the Deferred Revenue schedule, it's often due to manual journal entries made directly in the GL. Since these entries bypass the subledger logic, they will not appear in the schedule, leading to a mismatch.

To identify the cause of the discrepancy:

To pinpoint the issue:

  1. Export the Deferred Revenue GL detail from the Account Register.
  2. Filter the export by Manual JE Source.
  3. Review the entries to identify manual journal entries that may be causing the variance.

This approach will help you isolate the differences between the General Ledger and the subledger.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Closing the Books

Closing the Books

At the end of each book close period, you will need to lock editing to the month to prevent unwanted changes to the prior period financial information.

Closing the Books

To close the books, Navigate to Close Management > Checklist.

In the upper right corner, the latest open period will be displayed.

Click Close Books.

Reopening the Books

Within Close Management > Checklist, navigate to close period selector in the upper right corner. Select the prior month, click the More Options menu and select Reopen Books.

Reopening the books can only be performed by an Admin user.

  1. General Accounting
  2. Fixed Assets

Setting up Fixed Assets in Rillet

Rillet’s Fixed Asset module allows you to track, depreciate, and report on capitalized assets throughout their lifecycle. This guide outlines how to set up a fixed asset record, assign depreciation settings, and connect it to your accounting structure.

To set up your Fixed Asset Settings:

  1. Click your company logo in the lower-left corner
  2. Go to Organization Settings

  1. Select Accounting in the left menu
  2. Assign default information for existing fixed assets accounts
    1. Fixed Account: Automatically populates the accounts defined as fixed assets in the Chart of Accounts
    2. Accumulated Depreciation Account: The contra-asset account used to track depreciation to date
    3. Depreciation Account: The P&L account where depreciation expense is booked
    4. Useful Life: Number of months over which the asset will be depreciated (e.g., 36, 48, 60)
    5. Type:
      1. Even period: Equal depreciation per accounting period
      2. Daily: Prorated depreciation by service days in the period
  3. Assign default accounts to be used in the disposal of fixed assets.
    1. Gain/Loss on sale: The income statement account used to record any net gain or loss from the sale of an asset
    2. Sales proceed account: The account where cash or proceeds from the asset sale are deposited

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Fixed Assets

Creating a New Fixed Asset

Fixed assets represent long-term tangible assets used in business operations, such as equipment, furniture, and vehicles. In Rillet, creating a fixed asset record enables accurate tracking, depreciation scheduling, and financial reporting throughout the asset's lifecycle.

Before creating individual assets, ensure that default accounts for fixed asset types and depreciation settings have been configured by following the steps in the 📄 Setting up Fixed Assets in Rillet article.

Steps to Create a Fixed Asset

  1. Navigate to Accounts Payable > Bills.

  1. Select + Add New

  1. Enter standard bill information such as Vendor, Bill Date, Payment Terms
  2. Select a fixed asset account at the line item level. Once selected, additional fields populate to define the Fixed Asset terms
    1. Useful Life: Number of months the asset will be depreciated
    2. In Service Date: The date the asset was placed into use
    3. Asset ID (optional): An internal tracking ID, if applicable

  1. Click Save to generate the fixed asset bill

Once the fixed asset is saved, Rillet creates the corresponding bill and automatically schedules depreciation based on the provided inputs and organizational settings.

To view the schedule, find the corresponding bill and drill into the general ledger detail by selecting the three dots on the right side of the bill line item and select GL Impact.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Fixed Assets

Fixed Asset Bill Credits

When a vendor issues a refund or credit for a previously capitalized asset (e.g., return of equipment, invoice correction), you can record a bill credit in Rillet to reduce the value of the fixed asset and update the general ledger accordingly.

Steps to Create a Fixed Asset

  1. Navigate to Accounts Payable > Bills.

  1. Find the Original Bill

Locate the bill tied to the fixed asset you want to credit

  1. Open the Bill Credit Option

Click the Three Dots (...) and Select Bill Credit.

  1. Enter Bill Credit Details

In the pop-up window, enter the credit amount and set the applicable date

  1. Save the Credit Once saved, the asset will show as Partially Credited and the credit will be displayed automatically in the GL Impact.

Accounting and Depreciation Impact

Rillet automatically factors the bill credit into all future depreciation calculations. The remaining asset value is reduced by the credit amount, and depreciation will adjust accordingly from the credit date forward.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. General Accounting
  2. Fixed Assets

Fixed Asset Disposals

When a fixed asset is sold, retired, or written off, it must be formally disposed of in Rillet. This process ensures the asset is removed from the balance sheet, accumulated depreciation is cleared, and any gain or loss on the transaction is recorded.

Steps to Dispose of an Asset

  1. Navigate to Close Management > Fixed Assets.

  1. Locate the Asset

Find the fixed asset that you would like to dispose from the list, click on the three dots, then select Dispose

  1. Review the Asset Details

Within the pop-up window, confirm the asset information is accurate

  1. Enter Disposal Information
    1. Sale Date: The date of the sale or write-off
    2. Sale Value: Amount received, if any
    3. Tax: Enter tax percent or amount if applicable

  1. Confirm Disposal

Once the disposal details are complete, select Dispose

Notes:

  • Disposals are irreversible. Ensure all information is correct before proceeding.
  • Rillet will automatically calculate and display whether the disposal results in a net gain or loss.

Once disposed, Rillet will automatically post the resulting gain or loss to the appropriate general ledger account, as defined in your disposal settings, and reflect the transaction in the GL detail for full audit traceability.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


 

  1. Foreign Currency

Journal Entries in FX

What to do if you don't have unrealized fx gains/losses on transactions

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Journal Entries in FX

What to do if you don't have unrealized fx gains/losses on transactions

If you are expecting to have unrealized gain or losses on your invoices or bills, but are not seeing the correct transactions, review the following items:

Where to see gains/losses

On the Bill or invoice, you can view the Unrealized and Realized Gain/Loss impact in the bottom right corner of the bill:

Additionally, if you view the GL Impact of the transaction by selecting the 3 dot menu and select GL Impact, you can view the journal entries:

Other troubleshooting

Confirm that your setting are enabled correctly:

  • Multi-currency is enabled in the Advanced Settings
  • There is an unrealized G/L, realized G/L and a Revaluation account selected in the Accounting Settings
  • The Bill or Invoice Date is on or after the Revaluation Start Date (Accounting Settings)
  • The original bill or invoice is in a foreign currency

If all of these are confirmed, then please reach out to your Rillet team or support@rillet.com.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency

Bills in FX

Create a Bill with FXCreate Bill Credits with FXBill Payments with FX

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Bills in FX

Create a Bill with FX

This process enables users to manage transactions in different currencies while ensuring that all data is accurately recorded in the organization’s base currency.

To create bill with FX:

  1. Go to Accounts Payable > Bills.

  1. Select + Add Bill.

  1. Select the relevant currency when entering the bill data.
  2. To override the FX rate, click Edit Rate and input your desired FX rate.

  1. Click Save.

The bill will display in the transaction currency, and all journal entries will be recorded automatically in the organization’s base currency based on the FX rate applied.

PreviousBills in FXNextCreate Bill Credits with FX

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Bills in FX

Create Bill Credits with FX

Creating bill credits in a multi-currency environment allows for accurate adjustments to previously recorded invoices. This process ensures that foreign exchange considerations are handled properly.

To create bill credits with FX:

  1. Go to Accounts Payable > Bills.

  1. Locate the invoice you want to ajustar and click the invoice to open it.
  2. In the top right corner of the screen, click the three dots (...) and select Bill Credit.

  1. Complete the Bill Credit Form:
    • Vendor: Default(e.g., Apple).
    • Bill #: Default.
    • Date: Select the credit date (e.g., 04/09/2024).
    • Account: Default (e.g., Office Expenses & Supplies).
    • Amount: Enter the credit amount (e.g., €200.00).

Note: The credit must be entered in the same currency as the original bill and the Credit Amount must be ≤ Amount Due on the bill.

  1. Click the Save button to record the invoice credit.

Once saved, the relevant journal entries will be created in the organization currency.

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Bills in FX

Bill Payments with FX

In a multi-currency environment, managing bill payments involves two main options: Simple Matches and Complex Matches. Each method addresses different transaction scenarios, ensuring accurate handling of foreign exchange gains and losses.

Simple Matches

For direct one-to-one matches, apply the cash payment directly in the cash reconciliation.

  1. Go to Cash Reconciliation in the main menu.

  1. Select Bank Account.
  2. In the Bank Transactions panel, identify the related transaction, such asABC Company, Inc. on 02/23/2024 for -€1,000.00.
  3. In the Rillet Transactions section, look for the matching invoice. For example, the transaction from Slack on 12/15/2023 with the same amount -€1,000.00.
  4. Click the Multi Currency Match button.

Complex Matches

For partial or multiple matches, first create a Bill Payment on the cash payment date, then match it in the cash reconciliation.

Upon bill payment, journal entries will be recorded to update FX gains/losses from unrealized to realized.

  1. Go to Accounts Payable > Bills.

  1. Locate the invoice you want to pay and click the invoice to open it.
  2. In the top right corner of the screen, click the three dots (...) and select Pay Expense.
  3. In the Pay Expense window:
    1. Enter the amount (e.g., €500.00).
    2. Select the date (e.g., 02/23/2024).
    3. Ensure to choose the correct account, such as Euro Bank.
  4. Click Confirm to save the payment.

  1. Go to Cash Reconciliation in the main menu.

  1. Select Reconciliation Date.
  2. In the Bank Transactions panel, identify the related transaction, such as ABC Company, Inc. from 02/23/2024 for -€1,000.00.
  3. In the Rillet Transactions panel, look for the transactions corresponding to the payments made (e.g., -€541.62 from Slack).
  4. Click the Match button.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency

Set Up of FX Accounts

Set Up Accounts for FX Revaluation & Fx TranslationWhere does Rillet get FX Rates?Set Up Accounts for FX Revaluation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Set Up of FX Accounts

Set Up Accounts for FX Revaluation & Fx Translation

Foreign exchange (FX) revaluation is a necessary process for organizations that operate with multiple currencies. This procedure helps ensure that financial statements accurately reflect the current value of foreign currency transactions, including open invoices and vendor bills.

Set Up Accounts for FX Revaluation

To set up accounts for FX Revaluation:

  1. Click the company icon in the top right corner of the screen.
  2. Go to Organization Settings > Accounting.

Under the Multi-currency section, turn on the toggle to enable multi-currency functionality and input the required information.

Field Guide

AP & AR Revaluation Start Date

Define specific dates for foreign currency revaluation to begin, which is particularly important for accounts payable (AP) and accounts receivable (AR).

Note that these fields are not required unless you implemented Rillet prior to Jan 01 2024.

Realized FX Gain/Loss

Select an account from the chart of accounts to record realized gains or losses from foreign exchange transactions. This account will include all gain/losses on bills and invoices that are no longer open.

Unrealized FX Gain/Loss

Select an account for recording unrealized gains or losses, which are adjustments based on current exchange rates for transactions not yet settled.

Accounts Receivable Revaluation

Choose a dedicated account for reflecting any revaluation of accounts receivable due to changes in FX rates. This will be be a separate balance sheet account from Accounts Receivable.

Accounts Payable Revaluation

Select an account designated for revaluation of accounts payable in response to foreign exchange rate fluctuations. This will be be a separate balance sheet account from Accounts Payable

Cumulative Translation Adjustment

The Cumulative Translation Adjustment (CTA) is an entry in the equity section of a company's balance sheet that accounts for foreign currency translation gains and losses. It arises when a company has foreign subsidiaries whose financial statements are translated into the parent company’s reporting currency.

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Set Up of FX Accounts

Where does Rillet get FX Rates?

Rillet uses Open Exchange Rates (https://openexchangerates.org/) to pull daily exchange rates.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Foreign Currency
  2. Set Up of FX Accounts

Set Up Accounts for FX Revaluation

Foreign exchange (FX) revaluation is a necessary process for organizations that operate with multiple currencies. This procedure helps ensure that financial statements accurately reflect the current value of foreign currency transactions, including open invoices and vendor bills.

To set up accounts for FX Revaluation:

  1. Click the company icon in the top right corner of the screen.
  2. Go to Organization Settings > Accounting.

  1. Under the Multi-currency section, set up the following fields:
    1. Enable Multi-Currency: Ensure that multi-currency functionality is turned on to allow transactions in different currencies.
    2. Set Revaluation Start Dates: Define specific dates for foreign currency revaluation to begin, which is particularly important for accounts payable (AP) and accounts receivable (AR).
      1. AP revaluation start date (optional)
      2. AR revaluation start date (optional)

Note: If you wish to avoid recording revaluations prior to a certain date, you can set a start date. All bills dated before this date will not have unrealized gains or losses recorded, although realized gains or losses will still be captured upon payment. When a start date is set, all bills with a bill date prior to this date will not have unrealized gains/losses recorded, while realized gains/losses will still be accounted for upon payment.

    1. Choose an account for realized FX Gain/Loss: Select an account from the chart of accounts to record realized gains or losses from foreign exchange transactions.
    2. Choose an account for unrealized FX Gain/Loss: Select an account for recording unrealized gains or losses, which are adjustments based on current exchange rates for transactions not yet settled.

Note:

While the realized and unrealized accounts can be the same, it is advisable to differentiate them for better clarity in financial reporting.

    1. Choose an account for Accounts Receivable Revaluation (Balance Sheet): Choose a dedicated account for reflecting any revaluation of accounts receivable due to changes in FX rates.
    2. Choose an account for Accounts Payable Revaluation (Balance Sheet): Select an account designated for revaluation of accounts payable in response to foreign exchange rate fluctuations.
    3. Create Default FX Accounts (optional): Automatically generates default accounts for managing foreign exchange gains and losses, simplifying the setup process.
    4. Create Default CTA Account (optional): Enables the creation of a default Cumulative Translation Adjustment (CTA) account, used for translation adjustments due to changes in exchange rates.

  1. Click Save Changes.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Subsidiaries

Set up a New Subsidiary

A subsidiary is a separate legal entity owned or controlled by a parent company. Unlike the parent organization, which acts as an overarching structure, a subsidiary operates independently with its own financial records, legal obligations, and tax responsibilities.

When creating a new subsidiary, it is essential to establish it as a distinct entity within the organizational structure. This allows for:

  • Accurate financial tracking: Each subsidiary can maintain its own accounts and financial statements while still being consolidated under the parent company.
  • Legal and tax compliance: Subsidiaries must follow local regulations and tax requirements, which may differ from those of the parent organization.
  • Multi-currency support: A subsidiary can have its own functional currency, determining how transactions are recorded. However, financial consolidation may require currency conversions if the parent company reports in a different currency.

In many financial systems, subsidiaries are tagged in transactions such as contracts, journal entries, and invoices to ensure proper allocation. This distinction is critical for managing intercompany transactions, financial reporting, and compliance.

To create a new subsidiary, please reach out directly to your Rillet team or to support@rillet.com.

Set up your new subsidiary

To set up a new subsidiary:

  1. Click the company icon in the top right corner of the screen.
  2. Go to Organization Settings.

  1. In the Subsidiaries section in the left menu, choose an entity. Then, select Entity details.
  2. Complete the following fields in the Subsidiary Profile:
    • Logo: When uploading a logo or any additional image for the subsidiary, ensure you follow the requirements:mMaximum size: 1 MB, Minimum dimensions: 128 x 128 px, File format s: .png or .jpg
    • Legal Entity Name: Assign a unique legal name for the subsidiary (e.g., "Monsters, LLC").
    • Trade Name: Enter the trade name, which may be the same as the legal name in many cases.
    • Address Line 1: Provide the primary address (e.g., "123 Test Drive").
    • Address Line 2 (optional): This field can be used for additional address details if necessary.
    • Country: Select the country where the subsidiary operates (e.g., "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland").
    • City: Enter the city of the subsidiary's location (e.g., "London").
    • State (optional): Include the state if applicable. This may not be needed for all countries.
    • Zip Code: Provide the postal code for the subsidiary’s address (e.g., "123456").
    • Tax ID (optional): Include the tax identification number, if applicable, to comply with local regulations (e.g., "123456798").
    • Timezone: Specify the timezone the subsidiary operates in (e.g., "Etc/UTC").
    • Local Currency: Select the local currency that the subsidiary will use for its transactions (e.g., "GBP").

  1. Click Save Changes.

Accounting Settings

To configure the accounting settings:

  1. Select Accounting in the left menu.
  2. Define intercompany accounts to manage transactions between entities:
    • Receivable (Due From): The account recording amounts owed to the subsidiary (e.g., Due from UK). Typically an asset account.
    • Payable (Due To): The account recording amounts owed by the subsidiary (e.g., Due to UK). Typically a liability account.

  1. Click Save Changes.

Invoicing Settings

To configure the invoicing settings:

  1. Select Invoices in the left menu.
  2. Fill in the Invoicing Account Information. This is the account that will appear on invoices in the payment instructions.
    • Bank Name: Enter the bank's name where the subsidiary’s invoicing account is held.
    • Account Number: Input the account number for the subsidiary's bank account.
    • Routing Number: Specify the routing number necessary for processing payments.
    • Bank Address: Provide the address of the subsidiary’s bank.
    • Swift Code - International: Enter the international SWIFT code for cross-border transactions, if applicable.
    • Default Payment Terms: Set the default payment terms for invoices (e.g., "Due on Receipt").
    • Payment Instructions: Include specific instructions that customers should follow when making payments.
    • Show Bank Details on Invoices: Check the box if you want to display bank details on the invoices sent to customers.
    • Enable online payments for new customers (requires Stripe integration): If applicable, check the box to enable online payments for new customers.
  3. Check the option under the Taxes section to hide tax details on invoices if the total tax amount is $0.

FAQ

  1. Multi-Entity

Consolidation

Introduction to Multi-Entity ConsolidationConsolidated Financial Statements

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Consolidation

Introduction to Multi-Entity Consolidation

Managing financials across multiple entities can be a complex and time-consuming process for any organization. For companies operating across different geographies, currencies, and regulatory environments, having a streamlined approach to consolidate financial data is essential for accurate reporting, compliance, and strategic decision-making. This is where Rillet, with its powerful financial management capabilities, becomes an invaluable tool.

Rillet’s multi-entity consolidation feature enables businesses to simplify the management of multiple subsidiaries, entities, or business units. Whether your organization is managing intercompany transactions or consolidating financials in multiple currencies, Rillet provides a robust framework to bring it all together seamlessly.

Income Statement breakdown by entity

This series will cover the following key aspects of multi-entity consolidation in Rillet:

  • Setting Up Multi-Entity Structures: Understanding how to configure subsidiaries, entities, and intercompany relationships.
  • Intercompany Transactions: Exploring tools and features for managing intercompany transactions, including journal entries and eliminations.
  • Currency Management: Handling multi-currency operations, including exchange rate management and currency revaluation.
  • Financial Reporting: Creating consolidated financial statements and dashboards for a unified view of performance.
  • Best Practices: Tips and strategies for optimizing your consolidation process, troubleshooting common challenges, and ensuring data integrity.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Consolidation

Consolidated Financial Statements

Running consolidated financial statements in Rillet is very simple. Unlike other ERPs, Rillet is consolidates in real time so you never have to worry if your financial statements are up to date. There is no need to "run" the consolidation at month end since Rillet is always up to date.

Consolidated Financial Statements

By default, all financial statements are shown on a consolidated basis in the organization currency. For example when viewing the income statement, this statement is displayed at a consolidated level.

When viewing the transaction details, the line items will display the following information:

  • Subsidiary
  • Debit/Credit - Local Currency (the currency of the subsidiary)
  • Debit/Credit - Reporting Currency (the currency of the organization)

FX Translation

In order to convert from local to reporting currency, Rillet automatically calculates the FX Translation as follows:

  • Income Statement Accounts - Monthly Average FX Rate
  • Balance Sheet Accounts - End of Month FX Rate

The following are exceptions to these rules:

  • Equity Accounts - Historical FX Rate
  • Intercompany Accounts - Historical FX Rate
  • Retained Earnings
    • Net Income of each month is calculation using the Monthly Average FX Rate
    • Manual Adjustments to retained earnings will use the Historical FX rate.

Finally, the using these varied rates during the transactions process will result in the Cumulative Translation Adjustment in the Equity section of the balance sheet.

Breakdown by Subsidiary

To view a breakdown by subsidiary, take the following steps:

  1. Go to Reporting from the main menu.
  2. Select the Financial Statement that you want to view.
  3. Click Customize.

  1. Select Columns > Breakdown > Subsidiaries

Single Entity Financial Statements

To view your financial statements for one entity:

  1. Go to Reporting from the main menu.
  2. Select the Financial Statement that you want to view.
  3. Click Customize.
  4. Select the subsidiary that you want to view

This will display the financial statements of one subsidiary in the subsidiary currency.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Intercompany

Creating an Intercompany Account

An intercompany account is a key component in managing transactions between different entities within the same organization. These accounts are used to track and reconcile financial transactions between a parent company and its subsidiaries, or among subsidiaries themselves. Establishing these accounts correctly is crucial for maintaining accurate financial reporting and ensuring compliance with accounting standards.

What is an Intercompany Account?

An intercompany account is a general ledger account used to record financial transactions between different entities within the same corporate group. This includes sales, loans, transfers, and expenses that are exchanged between the parent company and its subsidiaries, or between subsidiaries themselves.

These accounts ensure that intercompany transactions are properly accounted for and eliminated during the consolidation process, preventing double-counting and providing a true picture of the group’s financial health.

Create an Intercompany Account

To create an intercompany account:

  1. Click the company icon in the top right corner of the screen.
  2. Go to Organization Settings > Chart of Accounts.

  1. Select + Add Account to open the account creation form.

  1. Complete the Account Information. For detailed instructions on filling out the account information, refer to the article 📄 Chart of Accounts

Fields include:

    • Number
    • Name
    • Type
    • Subtype
    • Parent Grouping (optional)

  1. Designate the account as Intercompany:
    • Toggle the switch to designate this account as an intercompany account. This is important for tracking transactions between different entities within the organization.
  2. Click Save to create the intercompany account.

Viewing Intercompany Accounts

Intercompany accounts will be designated with the Intercompany Icon on the Chart of Accounts:

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Intercompany

Create an Intercompany Entry

Intercompany entries differ from standard accounting entries as they involve transactions between different entities within the organization. Proper structuring is essential to maintain financial integrity and accurate reporting.

To create an Intercompany Entry:

  1. Go to Close Management > Intercompany.

  1. Select + Add Intercompany Entry.

  1. Fill in the required fields in the Entry form:
    • Name: Enter a unique description for the intercompany entry.
    • Date: Select the date for the transaction
    • Customer/Vendor (optional): Specify any associated customer or vendor, if applicable.
    • Currency: The currency will be the currency of the organization.
    • Attachment URL (optional): If relevant, add a URL for any supporting documentation.
    • Attach Files: Upload any relevant supporting documents.
  2. Enter the details for Entry Line Items :

1.                  From/To: The entity originating the transaction will be shown on the 'From' lines and the receiving entity will be shown on the 'To' lines.

2.                  Amount: Enter the amount for the transaction.

3.                  Account: Select the appropriate account(s).

4.                  Description (optional): Provide a brief description of the transaction.

5.                  To add additional line items, click + Add Lines.

  1. Click Create Entry to finalize the intercompany entry.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Multi-Entity
  2. Intercompany

Create an Intercompany Management Fee

An intercompany management fee is a charge that one entity within an organization imposes on another entity for management services provided. These fees typically cover administrative, operational, or strategic support services such as

Why Are Intercompany Management Fees Used?

  1. Cost Allocation: Distribute shared corporate expenses among group entities.
  2. Tax Optimization: Ensures that income is appropriately allocated across jurisdictions.
  3. Transfer Pricing Compliance: Many tax authorities require intercompany transactions to follow arm’s-length principles to prevent profit shifting.
  4. Transparency: Creates a clear record of services provided and the costs involved.

Before you start

Make sure the appropriate intercompany account exists. If not, follow the steps in📄 Creating an Intercompany Account

Create an Intercompany Management Fee

The walkthrough below is purely for example purposes. You will need determine the proper accounting treatment for your situation. If you are unsure of the proper treatment, please consult your accounting team or auditors.

In this example, Monsters Europe includes all administrative personnel. At the end of each month Monsters Europe charges the US and UK entities for their share of services, $20,0000 and $15,000 respectively, with a cost plus model.

Intercompany Accounts

In this example, we will use the following accounts:

  • Management Fee Revenue (Intercompany Account)
  • Management Fee (Intercompany Account)
  • Due From US (Intercompany Account)
  • Due From UK (Intercompany Account)
  • Due To EU (Intercompany Account)

Create the Intercompany Entry

To create the intercompany entry:

  1. Go to Close Management > Intercompany.

  1. Select + Add Intercompany Entry.

  1. Fill in the required fields in the Entry form:
    • Name: Management Fee
    • Date: Dec 31 2024
    • Customer/Vendor: blank
    • Currency: USD
  2. Detail the From Entry Line Items
    • Subsidiary: Monsters Europe
    • Amount: $35,000
    • Account: Management Fee Revenue

Enter the To Entry Line Items

4.                  Subsidiary: Monsters US

      1. Amount: $20,000.00.
      2. Account: Management Fee

5.                  Subsidiary: Monsters UK

      1. Amount: $15,000.00.
      2. Account: Management Fee
  1. Review and Save the Entry
    • Click Preview Entry to review the information entered.
    • Click Create Entry to save the intercompany management fee in the system, if everything is correct.

Intercompany GL Impact

  1. Integrations

Expense Management

Accessing Your Integrations Management PageConnecting Brex to RilletSet Up Expensify IntegrationExpensify Reimbursements & ChargesIntegrate Float to RilletRamp

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management

Accessing Your Integrations Management Page

You likely use a variety of software tools to manage your business in addition to Rillet. Good news: you can connect select tools to Rillet and sync data across platforms. Rillet supports integration with many providers across CRMS, Banks, HRIS, Spend Management, Bill Pay, etc.

To get started setting up one or multiple integrations, follow the steps below to access your integrations management page.

First, click on your company’s icon in the lower left corner. Then, click Organization Settings

You’ll be taken to a new screen where you can manage various settings for your company’s profile. In the left sidebar, find and click on the Integrations option.

This will take you to the central location for setting up, managing and disconnecting integrations. Each integration will appear as a tile under specific categories based on what they do.

From here, you can begin setting up your chosen integrations.

When you connect a specific integration, you’ll see it labeled as “Connected” on this screen. You’ll also see your connected integrations under the Tech Stack Monitoring section of your Launchpad.

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management

Connecting Brex to Rillet

Companies that use both Brex and Rillet can sync the two together in order to consolidate their finance management. In this article, we’ll explain the basics of Brex integration setup and walk you through the process of mapping data.

Connecting Brex to Rillet

First, click on your company’s icon in the lower left corner. Then, click Organization Settings.

In the next screen, click the Integrations tab in the left navigation sidebar.

Each integration option is displayed as a tile and organized under relevant categories. Find the one labeled Brex and click on it.

This will take you to a new screen, where you’ll see a button labeled Connect. Click on it.

You’ll be taken to the Brex homepage, where you’ll be prompted to log into your account. Once you’ve filled out your credentials, you’ll be taken back to Rillet and will be able to then set up your integration settings, which we’ll explain next.

Setting Up Your Brex Integration Settings

There are four steps involved in setting up Brex integration.

First, you’ll need to select the date you’d like to sync data from. The date you select will be the first day that Brex charge data will be synced from. You’ll also need to select a credit card payable account in this step. This account will appear on your balance sheet.

The second step is category allocation. You’ll see a list of categories provided by Brex on the left side, and on the right side you’ll see a list of GL accounts in Rillet. In this step, you’ll need to map each category to each appropriate GL account.

The third step is department mapping. Like the previous step, you’ll see a list of departments in Brex on the left side, and a list of departments in Rillet on the right side. Make sure all departments in both platforms are mapped appropriately and then continue to the final step.

The final step is location settings. It follows a similar mapping structure, so continue mapping as you did for the previous two steps. This step is optional if you do not use location.

Once you’ve completed all these steps, your integration will be almost ready to go. The final piece you’ll need to set up is connecting Brex as a bank connection. We’ll explain this in the next section.

Connecting Brex as a Bank Account

Return to your main Settings page and click on the Banks tab from your main navigation sidebar.

This will show you a list of all your connected bank accounts. Click the Add bank connection button in the upper right corner.

Follow the steps to connect Brex here as outlined in this article:

📄 Connect your Bank Accounts

Once you complete this step, your initial Brex integration has been set up. Continue reading to learn what changes in Rillet once you've integrated with Brex

How Does Brex Integration Work?

Once the integration between Brex and Rillet has been set up, vendors and charges in Brex will be synced automatically.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management

Set Up Expensify Integration

Rillet can integrate with Expensify to automatically create credit card charges and reimbursements in your accounting records. In this article we will walkthrough the steps to set up Expensify for syncing with Rillet.

Create Fields and Categories in Expensify

Categories

Categories are typically used in place of GL Account in Expensify. To modify and create categories, navigate to Expensify Settings > Workspaces, select the policy > Categories.

Fields

Fields in Expensify are useful for creating Departments or other custom information.

To create a new fields in Create Fields: Settings > Workspaces, select the related policy > Reports. At the bottom of the Reports tab, go to Add New Field

You can name the field anything, but you must select Type = Dropdown to be compatible with Rillet. Text and Date Field information will not be synced into Rillet.

Fields can only be created once you have disconnected Expensify from previous ERP integrations

Connect Expensify

In Rillet, navigate to Organization Settings > Integrations > Expensify

Click Connect. You will then be prompted to input the integration keys and start date

You can access your User ID and user secret in Expensify here on their Integration server page.

Once you have input the required information, Click Connect.

Mapping Expensify

There are a few steps to complete the mapping of Expensify Categories and Fields into Rillet.

General

  • Sync data from - This is the date the you'd like expenses to start syncing into Rillet
  • Charges Liability Account - This is typically an Expensify Clearing Account or a Credit Card Payable Account. Charges will be shown in this liability account until they are matched with the credit card bank feeds.
  • Reimbursement Liability Account - This is typically an Employee Reimbursement Payable Account to track any unpaid reimbursements.

Department Mapping (optional)

This step is optional, but needed if you'd like to sync Department information directly from Expensify.

On the left side will be the Expensify Policies and the Expensify Field information which will be mapped to the related Rillet Fields and values.

This mapping needs to be completed for each policy because Policies have separate Field sets in Expensify

Category Mapping

In the next step, you will mapping the Expensify categories (left) with the Rillet GL accounts (right). You can select the default account per policy and then make overrides for only the account which should be different.

Additional Field Mapping (optional)

In the final option step, you can choose to map additional custom Expensify fields into Rillet. This is common to be used if you'd like to track event expenses, project codes, or any other tags.

If you do not want to use this, you can leave this blank (shown below) and press Save

Once you've completed these steps, you'll need to press Save and your expensify integration is now set up and ready to sync!


 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management

Expensify Reimbursements & Charges

In this article we will review the Expensify data workflow. For guidance on setting up your Expensify Integration, please see this article:

📄 Set Up Expensify Integration

Expensify Charges Sync

Once a credit card charge has been added to the report and the report is in an Approved state, Rillet will sync in the related transaction as a Charge. All Charges can be viewed in Rillet by going to Accounts Payable > Charges.

Charge in Expensify Report view

Charge in Rillet

By default a charge will be imported as Unpaid. Once a charge has been imported, you can match the Charge with the related credit card transaction in the Rillet Bank Feed.

Expensify Reimbursements

Once a reimbursement has been added to the report and the report is in an Approved or Reimbursed state, Rillet will sync in the related transaction as a Reimbursement. All Charges can be viewed in Rillet by going to Accounts Payable > Charges.

Reimbursement Expense in Expensify

Reimbursement in Rillet

By default a reimbursement will be imported as Unpaid. Once a reimbursement has been imported, you can match the Charge with the related payor in the Rillet Bank Feed.

FAQ

  • What if the vendor does not already exist in Rillet?
    • If the Expensify vendor does not already exist, Rillet will automatically create a new vendor.
  • How can I see what Expenses have been synced?
    • You can quickly view which reports have been exported, in the Reviews listing view in Expensify.

  • When are transactions synced?
    • Transactions will only sync in once they are on a Report which is Approved or Reimbursed
  • How often do transaction sync?
    • Rillet will sync new approved or reimbursed reports every 1 hour.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Integrate Float to Rillet
  4. Integrating Float with Rillet enhances your account management by automating data synchronization and providing better visibility into your financials.
  5. To Integrate Float to Rillet:
  6. There are two ways to access the Float integration:
  7. Launchpad: Access the Float integration through the Browse Integration section from the Launchpad.
  9. Organization Settings:
  10. Click your company logo in the lower-left corner of the screen.
  11. Go to Organization Settings > Integrations.
  13. Under Accounts Payable, select Float.
  15. Complete the settings:
  16. Sync Data from: Choose the date from which you want Float to sync data. This helps ensure that the information is current and relevant.
  17. Choose existing account: If you already have Float accounts, select Choose Existing Account from the dropdown menu to pick a listed account.
  18. + Create new Float account: If you do not have an account or prefer to create a new one, select Create New Float Account. This action will automatically generate a new Float account for you.
  20. After filling in the necessary details, click the Save changes button to complete the integration setup.
  22. Once you save, you should see a confirmation that the setup was completed successfully, and the Float status will change from Not Connected to Connected.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Connect Ramp to Rillet

If your organization uses Ramp for corporate spending, you can integrate it with Rillet to sync your Ramp transactions automatically. This allows you to streamline your expense tracking, vendor billing, reimbursements, and card payments—all from within Rillet.

This article explains how to connect Ramp to your Rillet account and what happens after the connection is established.

Before You Begin

Ensure you have:

  • Admin access in both Rillet and Ramp.
  • Your Ramp account credentials.
  • The Ramp integration enabled in your Rillet environment.
  • Ramp disconnected from any previous accounting system.

Note: Ramp must be disconnected from any previous accounting system before connecting to Rillet. This prevents sync errors.

To Connect Ramp to Rillet:

There are two ways to access the Ramp integration:

  1. Launchpad: Access the Ramp integration through the Browse Integration section from the Launchpad.

  1. Organization Settings:
    1. Click your company logo in the lower-left corner of the screen.
    2. Go to Organization Settings > Integrations.

  1. In the Accounts Payable section, select Ramp > Not Connected.

  1. Click Connect Ramp and follow the login prompt.

After completing the initial Ramp login, you'll be redirected back to Rillet to complete setup.

  1. Continue to Complete the Ramp Setup.

For Multi-Entity Accounts

  1. Click your company logo in the lower-left corner of the screen.

  1. Select the organizational entity from the dropdown menu.
  2. Go to Organization Settings for that entity.

  1. In the left-hand menu, expand the Subsidiaries section and click the arrow to open the relevant subsidiary.

  1. Within the selected subsidiary, click Integrations in the left-hand menu.
  2. In the Accounts Payable section, select Ramp > Not Connected.

  1. Click Connect Ramp and follow the login prompt.

After completing the initial Ramp login, you'll be redirected back to Rillet to complete setup.

  1. Continue to Complete the Ramp Setup.

To Complete the Ramp Setup

Fill out the integration setup fields:

  • Sync data from: The first date Ramp transactions will sync into Rillet. You can backdate this if those transactions haven’t been synced to another accounting system.
  • Ramp entity (multi-entity only): Select the legal entity that pays your Ramp card balances and bills.
  • Reimbursement Vendor type: Choose whether reimbursements are assigned to:
  • The vendor name (e.g., Starbucks), or
  • The employee name (e.g., Grace Harris)
  • Ramp Credit Card Account: Select the GL liability account for your Ramp credit card balance.
  • Reimbursement Payable Account: Choose the GL liability account for employee reimbursements.
  • Mileage Vendor: Assign a vendor for mileage entries. You can select an existing vendor or create one called Mileage.
  • Mileage Expense Account: Select the GL account where mileage expenses should be recorded.

Once the setup is complete, Rillet syncs the following back to Ramp automatically:

  • GL Accounts
  • Custom Fields (e.g., Department, Location, Segment)
  • Accounting Vendors

Note: This data updates automatically in Ramp—no manual uploads required. Contact Rillet Support if you need help with the initial sync.

What Happens After Connection

After the integration is complete:

  • Credit card charges, vendor bills, and employee reimbursements will begin syncing into Rillet.
  • You’ll be able to manage mapped vendors, chart of accounts, and categories directly from within Rillet.
  • You can review and reset synced transactions at any time.

To learn how specific types of transactions sync, see How Ramp Transactions Sync into Rillet.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Manage Accounting Fields Across Rillet and Ramp

Rillet integrates seamlessly with Ramp to automatically sync accounting activity related to credit card charges, vendor bills, reimbursements, and Ramp card payments.

This article provides an overview of how this data flows from Ramp into Rillet and where it appears on the platform. To learn how each type of transaction syncs and how to work with it in Rillet, explore the articles linked below.

Transactions Synced from Ramp

Rillet pulls the following items from Ramp on an hourly basis:

  • Credit Card Charges: Charges made to Ramp cards (physical or virtual) sync to Rillet as payables once they are marked as Ready to Sync in Ramp. For more information, see Sync Credit Card Charges.
  • Vendor Bills: Once approved for payment, vendor bills in Ramp automatically sync into the Bills section in Rillet. For more information, see Sync Vendor Bills.
  • Employee Reimbursements: Reimbursements submitted and marked Ready to Sync will be added to the Vendors view in Rillet. For more information, see Sync Employee Reimbursements.
  • Ramp Card Payments: Monthly Ramp card payments made from your bank account will appear in your Bank Feed and automatically match the related Ramp charges. For more information, see Sync Ramp Card Payments.

What to Expect from the Sync Process

  • Transactions sync from Ramp into Rillet once per hour.
  • You can manually trigger a sync from Settings > Integrations > Ramp in Rillet.
  • Rillet avoids duplicate entries by using Ramp’s unique transaction IDs.
  • Changes made in Ramp (edits or deletions) will be reflected in Rillet on the next sync.

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Sync Credit Card Charges

When a transaction is made using a Ramp-issued card (physical or virtual), it can be synced into Rillet as a payable. This article explains how to prepare and sync credit card charges from Ramp.

Before You Begin

Ensure your Ramp integration is active in Rillet and that you’ve assigned GL Accounts and any required custom fields in Ramp. To view card transactions in Ramp, go to the Accounting section from the left navigation.

Mark Transactions as Ready to Sync

You must mark charges as Ready to Sync in Ramp before they can appear in Rillet.

To mark transactions in bulk:

  1. Go to Accounting > Card Transactions in Ramp.
  2. Select the checkbox at the top to Select all transactions.

  1. Click Mark Ready in the banner at the bottom of the screen.

To mark individual transactions:

  1. Fill in the required GL Account and classification fields.
  2. Click the Ready to Sync checkmark on the right side of the transaction row.

Once marked, the transactions will move to the Ready to Sync section. Only transactions in this section will be synced to Rillet automatically.

Where to Find Synced Charges in Rillet

Rillet pulls Ready to Sync charges from Ramp every hour. Once synced:

  • These charges appear in Accounts Payable > Charges

  • The Vendors tab is also updated with the associated vendor.
  • Click a charge to view more details, including a direct link back to the original Ramp transaction.

When a payment is made from your bank to cover a Ramp charge, Rillet automatically matches the transaction with your bank feed entry.

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Sync Vendor Bills

Vendor bills created in Ramp sync directly to Rillet once they’re approved for payment. This article explains how to manage synced bills and where to find them in Rillet.

Before You Begin

Before a vendor bill can sync into Rillet, confirm the following in Ramp:

  • The bill is marked as Approved for Payment.
  • A GL Account and any required custom fields (e.g., Department) have been assigned.

When Do Bills Sync?

Vendor bills are synced into Rillet automatically every hour, once they are approved for payment in Ramp. No additional action is required.

Where to Find Synced Bills in Rillet

After syncing, vendor bills appear in:

Accounts Payable > Bills

Each synced bill includes:

  • Vendor name
  • Bill amount and date
  • A direct link to the original bill in Ramp

Matching with Bank Payments

If you pay the bill through your connected bank account, the payment will appear in your Bank Feed and will be automatically matched to the bill in Rillet.

To learn more about matching, see: 📄 Matching and Unmatching Transactions with Rillet

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Cash Reconciliation
  2. Transaction Classification & Matching

Matching and Unmatching Transactions with Rillet

There are a few ways to match a bank account transaction to a previously created transaction in Rillet. The sections below will go over each method for matching.

One-to-One Matching

In the case that you need to match one bank transaction to one Rillet transaction, begin by clicking on your chosen bank transaction on the left side of your screen. Rillet will automatically populate some relevant options on the right hand side of your screen. Click See All if you want to see all eligible matching transactions.

When you select the Rillet transaction, a Match button will appear on the bottom right side of your screen.

From here, matching is simple: just click the Match button, and your transaction will move to the Matched section.

In case there isn’t an existing entry (which may happen with miscellaneous bank transactions or bank fees), you can create a quick entry by clicking the Quick Entry button in the upper right corner.

A modal will appear where you can quickly add a few details about your journal entry. You can add:

  • Payer type
  • Vendor
  • Account

You can also create a more complex JE with additional accounts if need be. Once you’ve filled out all your details, click Create and Match. The two transactions will now match and move to the Matched section.

One-to-Multiple Matching

It’s possible to match one bank transaction to multiple Rillet transactions. You can either match a bank transaction to an equal Rillet transaction, or link two different transaction amounts together with Rillet’s match and adjust feature.

Additionally, if you do not have a perfect match, you can use Match & Adjust to make small adjustments for bank fees or other small differences. Read this article to learn more about matching and adjusting: 📄 Matching and Unmatching Transactions in Rillet

To match one bank transaction to multiple Rillet transactions, follow the same steps as above to select your bank transaction and the Rillet transactions you’d like to match it to.

Unmatching a Transaction

Sometimes, you might create an incorrect match or quick entry. To undo this, you can unmatch a payment by simply clicking on the Unmatch option to undo the match or delete the quick entry.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Sync Employee Reimbursements

Employee reimbursements submitted through Ramp sync into Rillet automatically once they’re approved and marked as Ready to Sync. This article explains how to prepare reimbursements in Ramp and how to review them once they appear in Rillet.

Before You Begin

Before a reimbursement can sync into Rillet, ensure that:

  • A GL Account and any required custom fields (e.g., Department) are assigned in Ramp.
  • The reimbursement is marked as Ready to Sync under the Actions column.

Mark Reimbursements as Ready to Sync

To prepare reimbursements for sync:

  1. Go to Accounting > Reimbursements in Ramp.
  2. Select the appropriate GL Account and custom fields.
  3. In the Actions column, click Ready to Sync.

Once marked, the reimbursements will move to Ramp’s Ready to Sync section and will be pulled into Rillet automatically every hour.

Where to Find Synced Reimbursements in Rillet

After syncing, reimbursements appear under the vendor’s record in:

Accounts Payable > Vendors

Each synced reimbursement includes:

  • A Ramp label to help you identify its origin
  • A direct link to view the reimbursement in Ramp

Reimbursements will remain unpaid in Rillet until they are matched with the corresponding bank payment.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Sync Ramp Card Payments

Rillet automatically matches Ramp card payments with the charges they’re meant to cover. This article explains how Ramp payments appear in Rillet and how the matching works.

How Ramp Card Payments Are Synced

Once your organization pays the balance on a Ramp-issued credit card, the payment appears in the Ramp-linked Bank Feed as a positive amount.

Rillet automatically matches this deposit to the negative charges from the same credit card account.

This match usually happens as soon as both sides of the transaction are available in the feed.

Where to Find Matched Payments in Rillet

To review Ramp card payments in Rillet:

  1. Go to Cash Reconciliation > Bank Feed.
  2. Select the account linked to Ramp.

You’ll see:

  • The payment reflected as a deposit
  • The original charges that the payment offsets
  • The automatic match applied between them

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Integrations
  2. Expense Management
  3. Ramp

Ramp FAQs

This article addresses common questions about how Ramp integrates with Rillet and how synced transactions behave once they appear in your account.

Sync Behavior

How often do transactions sync into Rillet? Rillet pulls transactions from Ramp once every hour.

Can I update or reverse a transaction after it's been synced to Rillet? Once a transaction is synced from Ramp, it will not sync again. However, you can update the synced transaction directly in Rillet. Any changes made in Ramp after the initial sync will not overwrite or update the transaction in Rillet.

What date is used when syncing reimbursements? Rillet uses the Submitted date from Ramp when syncing reimbursements.

Attachments and Memos

Are memos or receipts from Ramp included when syncing transactions to Rillet? Yes. Receipts and memos added in Ramp are automatically attached to synced transactions in Rillet—including bills, credit card charges, and reimbursements.

What happens if a memo or receipt is added in Ramp after the transaction has already synced? Due to the one-time sync logic, receipts or memos added in Ramp after a transaction has synced will not appear in Rillet automatically. If needed, you can manually attach the memo or receipt in Rillet.

Vendors and Matching

Are new vendors created in Rillet if a Ramp transaction doesn’t match an existing vendor? Yes. If a transaction is synced from Ramp and there is no existing vendor match in Rillet (i.e., the Accounting Merchant field is blank in Ramp), Rillet will automatically create a new vendor record during the sync.

What happens when I pay a synced transaction through my bank? Rillet automatically matches synced Ramp transactions to payments in your Bank Feed. This applies to vendor bills, card charges, and reimbursements. You can review or manually unmatch these if needed.

Troubleshooting

What should I do if transactions are not syncing from Ramp to Rillet? Check the following:

  • In the Launchpad, verify if there are any alerts under Tech Stack Monitoring for your Ramp integration.
  • Confirm the transaction is marked as Ready to Sync in Ramp.
